summary: DHH describes his budget alternative to expensive 802.11b wireless networking as a poor student in Denmark. Rather than spending $500 on a wireless base station and card, he uses a long ethernet cable throughout his 60m apartment for just $10. He argues this low-tech solution works surprisingly well for computing from the couch, bed, kitchen, or dining table, and suggests the $490 savings could buy an iPod with change to spare.
key_points: 802.11b wireless networking costs ~$500 in Denmark circa 2001, making it impractical for students
A long ethernet cable provides the same mobility within a small apartment for $10
Practical, cheap solutions can match expensive tech for constrained use cases
The savings from choosing low-tech could fund other purchases like the newly released iPod
money_quotes: Being a poor student, my computing needs from the couch, bed, kitchen or dining table are meet the low-tech way: A really long cable!
Get a cable that matches the color of your walls, and it doesn't even look that geeky to visitors.
You could buy a iPod instead and still have change to spare.
key_insight: A $10 ethernet cable can solve the same problem as $500 wireless gear when you're willing to embrace a pragmatic, low-tech approach.
